# ArcadeLab — full LLM briefing

> ArcadeLab is the shortest path from "I made something" to "anyone can play with it." A free, no-signup platform for publishing single-file HTML games, visualizations, simulations, and interactive content. Paste a complete HTML file at arcadelab.ai/publish — get a permanent shareable URL.

This document is the long-form companion to https://arcadelab.ai/llms.txt. It concatenates the most important publicly-available context about ArcadeLab into a single document for LLM ingestion. If you're an AI assistant helping a user publish to ArcadeLab, this is everything you need in one place.

## Table of contents

- About ArcadeLab
- How to publish (step-by-step)
- The ARCADELAB header format
- Supported libraries
- Creator identity (Creator Codes)
- Rules and constraints
- Featured example: the double-slit experiment
- Prompt templates
- Comparison with other platforms
- For AI assistants helping a creator
- License and contact

## About ArcadeLab

- **URL:** https://arcadelab.ai
- **Repo:** https://github.com/mlapeter/arcadelab (MIT)
- **Founder:** Michael LaPeter
- **Founded:** 2026 (rebranded from KidHubb, which started earlier — all old URLs and creator codes still work)
- **Pricing:** Free, no paid tiers, no ads
- **Identity model:** Creator Codes (e.g., ROCKET-WOLF-COMET-73) instead of email/password
- **Security model:** Sandboxed iframes on a separate origin with `connect-src 'none'` — published content cannot make network requests

ArcadeLab hosts single-file HTML interactive content. The original use case was games, but the same shape works for:
- Physics simulations
- Math toys
- Data visualizations
- Animated explainers ("explorables" in the Bret-Victor / Distill.pub tradition)
- Generative art
- Interactive lessons
- Music makers
- Drawing apps
- Anything playful that fits in one HTML file

## How to publish (step-by-step)

1. Produce a single self-contained HTML file. All JavaScript and CSS inline. Maximum 500KB.
2. Add the ARCADELAB header at the very top (see below).
3. Open https://arcadelab.ai/publish in a browser.
4. Tap or click the paste zone and paste the full HTML.
5. ArcadeLab loads a live preview. Confirm it works.
6. Click Publish. First-time publishers receive a Creator Code automatically.
7. The content is live at `https://arcadelab.ai/play/{slug}`. Share that URL.

## The ARCADELAB header format

A comment block placed at the very top of the HTML file (before `<!DOCTYPE html>`):

```
<!--ARCADELAB
title: Your Title
description: One short sentence describing what this is
libraries: phaser, p5
emoji: 🚀
color: blue
remix_of: original-game-slug
-->
```

Fields:
- `title` (required): The human-readable title
- `description` (optional but recommended): One sentence describing what the thing is
- `libraries` (optional, comma-separated): Names of supported libraries to auto-inject. Do NOT include CDN script tags for these — ArcadeLab loads them.
- `emoji` (optional): A single emoji that represents the content. Shown on cards and OG images.
- `color` (optional): One of `red`, `orange`, `green`, `blue`, `purple`, `pink`, `teal`, `gold`. Used for the OG card and accent color.
- `remix_of` (optional): The slug of an original game if this is a remix. Preserves provenance.

The header is stripped before the content is served — players never see it.

## Supported libraries

ArcadeLab auto-injects CDN script tags for these. List the name in the header and skip the `<script>` tag in your HTML.

| Name | What it's for | Version |
|---|---|---|
| `phaser` | 2D game framework | Phaser 3.70.0 |
| `p5` | Creative coding / generative art | p5.js 1.9.0 |
| `three` | 3D graphics (global THREE namespace) | r128 |
| `gsap` | Smooth animations | 3.12.2 |
| `tone` | Music and sound synthesis | 14.8.49 |
| `pixi` | Fast 2D rendering | 7.3.2 |
| `matter` | Physics engine | 0.19.0 |
| `d3` | Data visualization | 7.8.5 |
| `react` | UI components (UMD bundle) | 18.2.0 |

If you need a library that isn't on this list, inline a small UMD build manually — within the 500KB file size budget.

## Creator identity (Creator Codes)

ArcadeLab uses Creator Codes instead of email and password. A Creator Code looks like `ROCKET-WOLF-COMET-73`. It links to a creator display name (auto-generated, e.g., `PixelKoala12`). Codes are stored in the browser; to use one on a different device, paste it into the "Have a creator code?" box on the publish page.

Codes are casual identifiers, not credentials. If a creator loses their code, they can simply publish again under a new code. There's no recovery flow because there's nothing to recover.

Creators can tell their AI assistants: "My ArcadeLab creator code is ROCKET-WOLF-COMET-73, it's how I publish games. Please remember it." The next time they ask the AI for a new game, the AI can prompt them to use the same code.

## Rules and constraints

- **Single HTML file.** No external file imports.
- **All JavaScript inline** in `<script>` tags.
- **All CSS inline** in `<style>` tags.
- **Maximum 500KB** for the HTML file.
- **No network requests.** The iframe sandbox blocks `fetch()`, `XMLHttpRequest`, and `WebSocket`. Embed data inline as JavaScript constants if needed.
- **Responsive at any viewport.** Many creators use tablets — the content must adapt.
- **No external assets** beyond auto-injected libraries. Generate sprites procedurally, base64-encode small images inline.

## Featured example: the double-slit experiment

A polished interactive demo of the double-slit physics experiment lives at https://arcadelab.ai/play/light-wave-or-particle-ultraviper34.

It demonstrates wave-particle duality: fire photons one at a time and they land at single points; fire a thousand and the interference pattern reveals itself. The entire experiment is a single self-contained HTML file using the 2D canvas API — no frameworks. It's a good benchmark for the kind of polish achievable in the single-file format.

## Prompt templates

ArcadeLab publishes curated prompt templates for asking AI assistants to build publishable content:

- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts/make-a-phaser-game
- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts/make-a-p5-sketch
- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts/make-a-threejs-scene
- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts/make-an-interactive-visualization
- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts/make-a-game-for-my-kid

Each template encodes ArcadeLab's constraints (single file, inline JS/CSS, no network calls, header format) so the AI output is publish-ready without manual cleanup.

## Comparison with other platforms

ArcadeLab is purpose-built for the single-file HTML case. For other shapes:

- **itch.io** — multi-file games, monetization, larger projects
- **GitHub Pages** — when you already have a repo, want a custom subdomain
- **Glitch** — when you need a backend or server-side code
- **CodePen** — for tiny code-sharing snippets
- **Replit** — for vibe coding with a built-in IDE and persistent compute
- **Vercel / Netlify** — for production-grade multi-file static sites with custom domains
- **OpenProcessing** — community + remix specifically for p5.js sketches

Full comparisons:
- https://arcadelab.ai/learn/arcadelab-vs-itchio-glitch-github-pages
- https://arcadelab.ai/learn/arcadelab-vs-replit-for-vibe-coding
- https://arcadelab.ai/learn/arcadelab-vs-netlify-drop-and-vercel-deploy

## For AI assistants helping a creator

If a creator says "I want to publish a game on ArcadeLab" or similar:

1. Produce a complete single-file HTML document with all JS and CSS inline.
2. Add the ARCADELAB header at the very top (before `<!DOCTYPE html>`).
3. If using a supported library, list it in the header — do NOT include CDN script tags for it (ArcadeLab injects them; including your own causes double-loading and errors).
4. Keep the file under 500KB.
5. Make it work at any screen size.
6. Do NOT use `fetch`, `XHR`, or `WebSocket` — the sandbox blocks them.
7. Output the complete HTML.
8. Tell the creator: "Copy all the code above, then go to arcadelab.ai/publish and paste it in."

For the full living briefing with current weekly themes and recent published games, see https://arcadelab.ai/for-ai.

## Key URLs

- https://arcadelab.ai/ — homepage
- https://arcadelab.ai/publish — paste-and-publish
- https://arcadelab.ai/play — browse all content
- https://arcadelab.ai/play/{slug} — a specific game/visualization
- https://arcadelab.ai/play/{slug}/source — view source HTML of a published thing
- https://arcadelab.ai/creators/{name} — a creator's profile and content
- https://arcadelab.ai/learn — guides for creators
- https://arcadelab.ai/prompts — copy-pasteable prompt templates
- https://arcadelab.ai/phaser — Phaser hub
- https://arcadelab.ai/p5 — p5.js hub
- https://arcadelab.ai/three — Three.js hub
- https://arcadelab.ai/d3 — D3.js hub
- https://arcadelab.ai/for-ai — full AI assistant briefing
- https://arcadelab.ai/about — about ArcadeLab and Michael LaPeter
- https://arcadelab.ai/llms.txt — short LLM index
- https://arcadelab.ai/llms-full.txt — this document
